steps.action: Sirtaki or syrtaki (Greek: συρτάκι) is a dance o with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Sirtaki or syrtaki (Greek: συρτάκι) is a dance o with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: It is a recent Greek folkdance, and a mixture of
steps.text: It is a recent Greek folkdance, and a mixture of "syrtos" and the slow and fast rhythms of the hasapiko dance.

steps.action: The dance and the accompanying music by Mikis Th
steps.text: The dance and the accompanying music by Mikis Theodorakis are also called Zorba's dance, the Zorba or "the dance of Zorba".

steps.action: The dance has become popular in Greece and is id
steps.text: The dance has become popular in Greece and is identified with the Greeks, becoming an invented tradition.

steps.action: The dance is therefore an amalgamation of three
steps.text: The dance is therefore an amalgamation of three different dance styles.

steps.action: It also has some characteristics of the zeybek d
steps.text: It also has some characteristics of the zeybek dance.

steps.action: The choreography of the dance for the film has b
steps.text: The choreography of the dance for the film has been attributed to Giorgos Provias.

steps.action: The zeibekiko was initially omitted, as the dire
steps.text: The zeibekiko was initially omitted, as the director felt it was not a suitable dance for a non-Greek and that the hasapiko looked better on screen.

steps.action: The dance was named Sirtaki at the first screeni
steps.text: The dance was named Sirtaki at the first screening of the film.

steps.action: In Stamatis Kokotas' 1966 song "Στου Όθωνα τα χρ
steps.text: In Stamatis Kokotas' 1966 song "Στου Όθωνα τα χρόνια" ("In the days of King Otto") there is an anachronistic reference to the king's Bavarian bodyguards dancing sirtaki.

steps.action: When performing alone, the sirtaki-dancer raises
steps.text: When performing alone, the sirtaki-dancer raises his arms horizontally on both sides of the body.

steps.action: Accordingly, the dance begins with slower, smoot
steps.text: Accordingly, the dance begins with slower, smoother actions, gradually transforming into faster, vivid ones, often including hops and leaps.

steps.action: Despite its name, sirtaki incorporates both syrt
steps.text: Despite its name, sirtaki incorporates both syrtos (in its slower part) and pidikhtós (in its faster part) elements.

steps.action: Quinn could not perform the fast part of choreog
steps.text: Quinn could not perform the fast part of choreography planned for the final scene by the choreographer, and the choreography had to be changed on the spot.

steps.action: It is claimed that this explains the crane shot
steps.text: It is claimed that this explains the crane shot moving away from the protagonists at the end of the film.

steps.action: Parts of the leg movements may be suggestive of
steps.text: Parts of the leg movements may be suggestive of crushing of grapes.

steps.action: Meter is 44, tempo increasing, and often the sig
steps.text: Meter is 44, tempo increasing, and often the signature is changed to 2/4 in the fastest part.

steps.action: The Association for Social Impact and Culture to
steps.text: The Association for Social Impact and Culture to the Municipality of Magnisia organized the event on the main beach of the town of Volos under the light of the second full moon of the month.

steps.action: Some 5,614 people, aged from 14 to 89, danced to
steps.text: Some 5,614 people, aged from 14 to 89, danced to the music of Mikis Theodorakis' Zorba the Greek, filling the town square and making it into the Guinness World Records.

steps.action: Enthusiasts from Volos, Larisa, Athens, Thessalo
steps.text: Enthusiasts from Volos, Larisa, Athens, Thessaloniki, Trikala, and the Greek islands joined the regional union of blind people, the national synchronized swimming team and many ordinary citizens.

steps.action: The idea came from a Volos resident, Alexia Halv
steps.text: The idea came from a Volos resident, Alexia Halvatzakou, who suggested it to the municipal services as an alternative way to promote the area.

steps.action: The President of the Association for Social Impa
steps.text: The President of the Association for Social Impact and Culture, Costas Halevas, liked the idea and set about organizing the event.
